verses.text: David weeps so bitterly for Absalom that the victorious army creeps back into the city ashamed, as if defeated. The king's private grief has swallowed the people's deliverance. Even right sorrow, left unchecked, can wrong those who risked all to save us.

Joab rebukes David hard: you shame the men who saved your life by loving those who hate you; rise and speak to them, or all will desert you. The words are harsh but true. Sometimes a blunt friend must recall a grieving leader to his duty before his people.

Throughout the tribes the people argue: the king who saved us is in exile — why are we slow to bring him back? Hearts that had strayed to Absalom now remember David's worth. Adversity past, men weigh again who truly delivered them.

Throughout the tribes the people argue: the king who saved us is in exile — why are we slow to bring him back? Hearts that had strayed to Absalom now remember David's worth. Adversity past, men weigh again who truly delivered them.

David, rather than force his return, sends to the elders of Judah, his own tribe: why should you be last to bring back the king? He woos rather than commands. A wise leader heals division by appeal and patience, not by demanding his rights.

David, rather than force his return, sends to the elders of Judah, his own tribe: why should you be last to bring back the king? He woos rather than commands. A wise leader heals division by appeal and patience, not by demanding his rights.

David even offers Amasa, Absalom's own general, command of his army, and so wins back Judah's heart; they invite him over the Jordan. He overcomes a rival not by vengeance but by generous trust. Grace extended to a former enemy can turn rebellion into loyalty.

Shimei, who had cursed the fleeing king, now hurries to meet him, falls down, and confesses, 'I have sinned.' The man bold to curse in David's weakness is quick to grovel in his strength. Fear of consequences is not the same as a changed heart, yet David hears him out.

Shimei, who had cursed the fleeing king, now hurries to meet him, falls down, and confesses, 'I have sinned.' The man bold to curse in David's weakness is quick to grovel in his strength. Fear of consequences is not the same as a changed heart, yet David hears him out.

Abishai urges that Shimei die for cursing the LORD's anointed, but David refuses — this is a day of restoration, not revenge — and swears Shimei shall not die. On the day of his own deliverance, the king deals in mercy. Grace received makes a heart slow to destroy.

Mephibosheth comes unkempt, having mourned since David left, and explains that Ziba slandered and deceived him. The lame man's neglected appearance proves his loyalty was real. Truth, long misjudged, comes out at last; the falsely accused is vindicated when the king returns.

Mephibosheth comes unkempt, having mourned since David left, and explains that Ziba slandered and deceived him. The lame man's neglected appearance proves his loyalty was real. Truth, long misjudged, comes out at last; the falsely accused is vindicated when the king returns.

David, unsure whom to believe, divides the land between Ziba and Mephibosheth; but Mephibosheth says, 'let him take all,' content simply that the king is home safe. His love for David outweighs any claim to land. A heart that treasures the king cares little for the estate.

Old Barzillai, who fed David in exile, comes to escort him over Jordan, and David invites him to court. But the eighty-year-old declines: his senses are dulled, and he would only burden the king. Honest age knows its limits and does not grasp at honours it can no longer enjoy.

Old Barzillai, who fed David in exile, comes to escort him over Jordan, and David invites him to court. But the eighty-year-old declines: his senses are dulled, and he would only burden the king. Honest age knows its limits and does not grasp at honours it can no longer enjoy.

Barzillai asks only to die in his own city by his parents' graves, and sends his servant Chimham in his place. He seeks no reward for his kindness, content to have served. The truest generosity gives without angling for repayment, and lets others carry forward the blessing.

Barzillai asks only to die in his own city by his parents' graves, and sends his servant Chimham in his place. He seeks no reward for his kindness, content to have served. The truest generosity gives without angling for repayment, and lets others carry forward the blessing.

Barzillai asks only to die in his own city by his parents' graves, and sends his servant Chimham in his place. He seeks no reward for his kindness, content to have served. The truest generosity gives without angling for repayment, and lets others carry forward the blessing.

David promises to do for Chimham whatever Barzillai wishes, kisses the old man, blesses him, and crosses over. Kindness shown to the king in his exile is now repaid to his household. God does not forget the cup of water given to His servant in the day of trouble.

No sooner is the king back than Israel and Judah quarrel bitterly over who has the greater claim to him. The joy of restoration curdles into rivalry and pride. Even a good homecoming can be spoiled when men contend for honour instead of rejoicing together.
